metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the 'nit-framing experiment' inline in the plan (or reference a named prior incident): describe the scenario, the fixture content or schema, and the specific scorer assertion (e.g., no-fire on X prompt-id when input is Y).
findings.finding: AC-5 references 'the nit-framing experiment' as if it is a known artifact, but nowhere in the plan is this experiment defined — no fixture content, no description of the nit scenario, no scorer assertion spelled out. An executor cannot author the fixture or write the scorer assertion without knowing what the experiment is.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief subsection under 'What' or a Note under AC-5: 'Nit-framing experiment: a case where a defect is described as a nit/suggestion rather than a hard failure; the base prompt should still fire the seeded defect. Fixture: diff containing [describe], prompt-id: code-review-base, expected: findings includes entry matching [pattern].'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the nit-framing experiment in the plan body (or reference a ticket/document): what prompt behaviour was tested, what the fixture input/output looks like, and what the expected no-fire/recall outcomes are.
findings.finding: AC5 references 'the nit-framing experiment' as a committed eval case (fixture + scorer assertion), but this experiment has no presence anywhere in the codebase and is not described in the plan body. The plan provides no specification of what the nit-framing experiment is, what its fixture looks like, or what scorer assertion it requires — making this an unspecified CREATION scope item.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the scoring protocol for `code-review-verify` cases: specify the `expect` vocabulary (e.g. an index-keyed `verified_finding_N` or a case-level `expect: high_validity` with an explicit `finding_index` field), and register a scorer that extracts the right verification item from the `verifications` list before applying `_validity`.
findings.finding: The scoring contract for `code-review-verify` cases is unspecified: the plan says verify returns `{verifications:[...]}` (a list of per-finding verifications) but does NOT define (a) what `expect` vocabulary a verify eval case uses, (b) what scorer function scores a verifications-list output (the existing `_validity` scorer extracts a scalar from a single-item dict, not from a list), or (c) how the scorer selects the right verification item by finding index. This is a schema/protocol gap — a verify case cannot be scored without this.
findings.location: ## What — OUTPUT-SHAPE CONTRACT; AC line 2 (code_review_* scorer); eval_scorers.py

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm AC-2's 'new code_review_* gating scorers' means adding REGISTRY aliases pointing to the existing _recall/_no_fire/_schema_review_result implementations (not new scorer functions) — the existing implementations already handle {findings:[...]} outputs correctly, so rebuilding them would be NIH.
findings.finding: AC-2 calls for 'new code_review_* gating scorers' but the existing REGISTRY already contains functionally identical scorer functions (_recall, _no_fire, _schema_review_result) registered under aliases like recall_on_seeded_defects, no_fire_on_good_cases, emits_valid_findings — all of which already operate on {findings:[...]} shaped outputs. Writing new scorer implementations for code-review would rebuild logic already present and tested.
findings.location: AC line: '>=1 code_review_* gating scorer registered that scores on findings presence/absence'; eval_scorers.py REGISTRY lines 336-362
findings.suggested_fix: In the implementation note for AC-2, replace 'register code-review gating scorers' with 'add code_review_* REGISTRY aliases for the existing _recall, _no_fire, and _schema_review_result scorers' to make the intent unambiguous and prevent a NIH reimplementation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan whether the new `code_review_*` scorer registrations are needed because the verify arm's `{verifications:[...]}` shape (which has no `findings` key) genuinely requires a new scorer, while base/overlay arms can reuse existing `recall_on_seeded_defects`/`no_fire_on_good_cases` — and if so, scope AC #2 to the verify arm only; otherwise justify why separate registrations are needed for base/overlay despite `_fired()` already handling the `{findings:[...]}` shape.
findings.finding: The plan asserts new `code_review_*` scorers must be registered because existing `_recall`/`_no_fire` scorers would produce a 'vacuous-pass bug' for base/overlay outputs — but `_fired()` in eval_scorers.py already handles `{findings:[...]}` outputs with no `verdict` key correctly (falls through to `bool(_findings(out))` at line 79). The novel-scorer requirement is therefore unverified: the plan does not explain why `recall_on_seeded_defects` / `no_fire_on_good_cases` (which delegate to the same `_fired()`) are insufficient for base/overlay eval cases, making it unclear whether truly new scorer registrations are architecturally necessary vs. simply reusing existing ones with new YAML names.
findings.location: What / OUTPUT-SHAPE CONTRACT section; AC #2 ('>=1 code_review_* gating scorer registered'); eval_scorers.py lines 73-79 (_fired), lines 187-200 (_recall, _no_fire)
findings.suggested_fix: Revise AC #2 to: '>=1 code_review_verify_* gating scorer registered that scores on verifications presence/absence for the verify arm; base/overlay recall/no-fire cases reuse `recall_on_seeded_defects`/`no_fire_on_good_cases` (which already handle `{findings:[...]}` outputs via `_fired()`).' This makes the new-scorer scope precise and avoids duplicating existing logic.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the case-field input contract for the new `run_case` code-review arm: which case fields feed `diff_text`/`ticket_context` (the assembled diff the base/overlay prompts receive), `changed_files`, and fixture `files` (the repo content the agentic prompts' file tools read).
findings.finding: The plan specifies the new `run_case` code-review arm's output shape but never specifies the INPUT mapping: what case fields provide the diff/context that the agentic base and overlay prompts consume as `ticket_context`. The existing arms each have explicit case-field contracts (e.g., `ticket_context`, `ticket_type`, `epics`, `spec`); the code-review arm has none stated. Without this contract, an implementer has no spec for what a valid eval case YAML field set looks like — they may use an incorrect field name (e.g., `input` instead of `diff_text`) producing silently empty context and vacuously passing no-fire cases.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'Harness prerequisite' or AC-1 section: 'A code-review eval case carries: `diff_text` (the unified diff, injected as `ticket_context`), optional `changed_files` (derived from `diff_text` if absent), and optional `files` (fixture repo content the agentic reviewer's file tools read). The arm assembles `DiffContext` from these fields and seeds `case_store` with `files` before running the prompt.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the code-review eval case fixture schema (at minimum: which keys the arm reads — `diff_text`? `files`? `changed_files`? — and whether it invokes a single prompt directly or uses the workflow engine), co-located with the run_case arm description.
findings.finding: The `run_case` code-review arm fixture-input schema is never defined: the plan says to add the arm but does not specify what keys a code-review eval case carries (e.g. `diff_text`, `files`, `changed_files`, `prompt_id` dispatch) — unlike completion-verifier (`ticket_context`+`files`) and spec-alignment (`epics`+`spec`), which have fully enumerated case schemas. An implementer must guess whether to seed a git fixture, pass `diff_text` inline, or call the full workflow vs. a single-prompt invocation.
findings.location: ## What — Harness prerequisite / eval_solver.run_case arm; AC line 1
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan: 'A code-review eval case carries `diff_text` (inline unified diff) and optionally `files` (path→content dict for fixtures); the arm seeds a disposable git repo (reusing `case_store`), writes `files`, and invokes the target prompt directly (not the full workflow gate) with the diff as context — returning the prompt\'s native structured output.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the harness prerequisite section that the new code-review run_case arm feeds diff context via `case.get('diff_text')` passed to `assemble_diff_context(diff_text=...)` — this reuses the existing offline seam rather than inventing a new git-diff fixture path.
findings.finding: The plan's new run_case arm for code-review prompts needs to supply a `diff_text` (or `changed_files`) to the prompt — but the existing `case_store` seam sets up a git repo with committed fixture files and has no mechanism to extract or supply a diff. The plan states the arm 'runs INDIVIDUAL prompts (base/overlays return {findings:[...]}; verify returns {verifications:[...]})' but does not specify how the diff context is fed from the eval case dict to the prompt invocation.
findings.location: Plan section 'What — Harness prerequisite'; eval_solver.py case_store / run_case; code_review/assemble.py assemble_diff_context
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence: 'Each code-review eval case carries a `diff_text` key (a minimal unified diff fixture); run_case passes it to assemble_diff_context(diff_text=case["diff_text"])  — the existing offline seam, no git range needed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that specifies a concrete proving command (e.g., `pytest src/rebar/llm/evals/ -k code_review` or `rebar eval --suite code_review`) whose passing output demonstrates at minimum: all seeded no-fire cases score 0 and all seeded recall cases score 1 against the deterministic scorer.
findings.finding: No AC or step specifies a concrete proving command that runs the new eval harness and asserts the seeded no-fire and recall cases actually produce the expected outputs. AC-3 ('eval specs exist') and AC-4 ('seeded from dso incidents') verify file/dataset presence but not correctness of execution — a syntactically present but broken spec satisfies both ACs.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC-3, AC-4; What section
findings.suggested_fix: Replace or augment AC-3/AC-4 with: 'Running [eval CLI/pytest command] against the seeded suite produces: recall cases — all seeded defect types flagged (score=1); no-fire cases — zero false positives (score=0). Output is captured in CI artifacts.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or explicitly scope-out with rationale) for the conditional _fired()/BLOCK extension described in the OUTPUT-SHAPE CONTRACT paragraph — either 'AC6: if a full-gate case is authored, _fired() matches BLOCK and FIRE_EXPECTS includes block' or 'Out of scope for this story; deferred to gate story.'
findings.finding: The 'What' section conditionally describes extending _fired() to match verdict=='BLOCK' and adding 'block' to FIRE_EXPECTS as a work item, but no Acceptance Criterion covers this conditional deliverable — it is orphan described work with no verifiable done-definition.
findings.location: What section (OUTPUT-SHAPE CONTRACT paragraph) vs Acceptance Criteria (AC1–AC5)
findings.suggested_fix: Add: '- [ ] (Conditional) If any eval case exercises the full-gate verdict, _fired() is extended to match verdict=="BLOCK" and FIRE_EXPECTS includes "block"; dataset validation accepts it.' Or explicitly mark it out-of-scope in the Scope section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the contradiction between 'No gate behavior change' (Scope) and the conditional _fired()/BLOCK extension (What): either confirm no existing case returns BLOCK (and document it), or guard the BLOCK match behind a code-review-specific scorer rather than modifying shared _fired().
findings.finding: The Scope section declares 'No gate behavior change,' but the What section describes modifying eval_scorers.py's _fired() function to additionally match verdict=='BLOCK' — a change to shared scoring logic that could alter how existing non-code-review cases are scored if any return a BLOCK verdict today.
findings.location: Scope section ('No gate behavior change') vs What section (OUTPUT-SHAPE CONTRACT, '_fired() currently matches only verdict==FAIL; … extend _fired to also match verdict==BLOCK')
findings.suggested_fix: Implement BLOCK matching in a new code_review_fired() helper used only by code-review scorers, leaving the shared _fired() unchanged — this satisfies both the no-gate-behavior-change constraint and the new scoring need.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit back-out plan for the _fired/FIRE_EXPECTS extension (e.g. keep the BLOCK match behind a feature flag or document a one-line revert diff) and verify no existing fixture produces verdict=='BLOCK' before merging.
findings.finding: The conditional extension to _fired() (adding verdict=='BLOCK' match) and FIRE_EXPECTS (adding 'block') lacks any rollback or back-out plan. If existing eval cases happen to carry verdict=='BLOCK' outputs, they would start firing after this change, silently altering existing eval results — contradicting the stated 'No gate behavior change' invariant. No expand-contract sequence or revert path is described.
findings.location: OUTPUT-SHAPE CONTRACT section / eval_scorers.py _fired() / AC line 2
findings.suggested_fix: Before widening _fired, add a one-line assertion/grep in CI confirming no existing fixture file contains '"verdict": "BLOCK"'; document the revert as: 'remove the `verdict=="BLOCK"` branch and `"block"` from FIRE_EXPECTS — safe because code-review scorers never call _fired'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the findings-based vs verdict-based scoring split (and the 'do NOT route code-review cases through _fired' rule) in a CLAUDE.md section or a lightweight ADR in the eval_specs/ directory so future contributors have a durable reference.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural contract (findings-based scoring decoupled from verdict-based scoring, with a new run_case arm and structured output shapes {findings}//{verifications}) that crosses eval_solver, eval_scorers, and eval_specs components, but does not call for capturing this decision in an ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design-doc update.
findings.location: OUTPUT-SHAPE CONTRACT section / Scope (eval_solver.py, eval_scorers.py, eval_specs/)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `eval_specs/ADR-001-code-review-scorer-contract.md` (or a CLAUDE.md section) with: (1) the two scorer families and when each applies, (2) the _fired boundary, (3) the {findings}/{verifications} output shapes. Link it from eval_scorers.py's module docstring.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify error handling for the new run_case code-review arm: define behavior on LLM timeout/non-200 (surface as a distinct `error` result, not a pass/fail), and add explicit JSON-schema validation of the response shape ({findings:[...]}/{verifications:[...]}) so shape mismatches are caught and logged rather than silently misscored.
findings.finding: The new eval_solver.run_case code-review arm makes LLM calls but the plan specifies no error handling: what happens when the LLM times out, returns a non-200, or returns JSON that doesn't conform to the expected {findings:[...]} / {verifications:[...]} shape? A malformed response will propagate as an unhandled exception, silently aborting an eval run and potentially masking a recall failure as an infrastructure error rather than a scoring outcome.
findings.location: Scope: src/rebar/llm/evals/eval_solver.py (new code-review arm) / AC line 1
findings.suggested_fix: In the new arm, wrap the LLM call in a try/except that catches timeout and HTTP errors and returns a sentinel result (e.g., `{'error': str(e), 'findings': []}`) that the scorer maps to a hard FAIL with a logged diagnostic. Add a pydantic/jsonschema parse step immediately after receiving the response; if parsing fails, raise a descriptive `EvalShapeError` rather than propagating a KeyError deep in scorer logic.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in AC5 that the nit-framing case's no-fire/recall labels are human-determined ground-truth assertions (derived from dso incident analysis, not from running the current gate), so the eval detects future regressions rather than snapshotting current behavior.
findings.finding: AC5 requires that the nit-framing eval case's 'no-fire/recall expectations pass on the current gate as baseline,' but the plan does not clarify whether these pass/fail labels are determined by human-labeled ground truth from the dso corpus or simply by recording whatever the current gate currently does. If the latter, this is a self-authored-oracle / change-detector anti-pattern: the test will snapshot current (possibly wrong) model behavior as ground truth, locking in any existing defect in nit-framing handling.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5: 'its no-fire/recall expectations pass on the current gate as baseline'
findings.suggested_fix: Amend AC5 to read: 'The nit-framing experiment is encoded as a committed eval case with human-labeled expected outcomes (no-fire or recall, per dso incident analysis); the case is asserted against those labels on the current gate to confirm the gate already handles nit-framing correctly, and any future prompt change must continue to satisfy the same human-labeled expectations.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm (by reading the verifier's JSON schema or contracts file) that the code-review-verify prompt's raw structured output uses a top-level `verifications` key as a list, and update the plan's OUTPUT-SHAPE CONTRACT or the scorer design accordingly if it differs.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'verify returns {verifications:[...]}' as the individual-prompt output shape, but the code-review-verify.md frontmatter declares `outputs: verification` (singular), and the workflow consumer (workflow_ops.py line 269) reads `ctx.inputs.get('verifications')` from a collect/fan-in step — not directly from the verifier prompt's raw output dict. The actual top-level key in the verifier's structured JSON output is unconfirmed from the plan text alone.
findings.location: What / OUTPUT-SHAPE CONTRACT section; AC line 1 ('verify returns {verifications:[...]}')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `eval.py`'s `validate_dataset_and_gold` (which imports `FIRE_EXPECTS`/`ALLOWED_EXPECTS`) as a consumer requiring review if 'block' is conditionally added to `FIRE_EXPECTS`, and confirm the updated balance-check logic is intentional.
findings.finding: validate_dataset_and_gold in eval.py (outside the plan's stated scope dir) imports and uses FIRE_EXPECTS and ALLOWED_EXPECTS from eval_scorers. The plan's conditional extension of FIRE_EXPECTS to include 'block' would change this function's balance-check logic and dataset validation behavior, but eval.py is not enumerated as a consumer requiring review.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in the Scope section that eval.py (validate_dataset_and_gold / validate_eval_spec) is an affected consumer; verify that the strict-mode balance check handles 'block'-only fire axes correctly (or add 'block' only to ALLOWED_EXPECTS, not FIRE_EXPECTS, if full-gate recall cases must still pair with a pass case).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive rationale in the harness prerequisite section explaining why the code-review arm uses the `case_store` disposable-repo path: the base/overlay prompts are `execution_mode: agentic` and call file tools on the fixture repo, requiring a real git checkout — not the inline-text path used for plan-review criteria.
findings.finding: The plan does not state a positive rationale for using the existing disposable-`case_store` / fixture-repo pattern (vs. an inline-text seam) for the code-review eval arm, even though the choice is non-obvious: code-review prompts are explicitly agentic and call file tools on the fixture repo, which is precisely what `case_store` provides. A reader of the plan cannot confirm the approach is correct without independently checking the prompt's `execution_mode: agentic` declaration.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Co-locate the `_fired`/`FIRE_EXPECTS` extension rule with a case-schema field (e.g. `mode: full_gate`) that identifies when a case exercises the full-gate verdict path vs. the per-prompt findings path, so the scorer can dispatch correctly.
findings.finding: The plan's `_fired` / `FIRE_EXPECTS` extension rule for full-gate-verdict cases (add `verdict=='BLOCK'` match and `'block'` to `FIRE_EXPECTS`) is stated as a conditional ('if and only if a case exercises the full-gate verdict') but the decision rule for WHEN a case exercises the full gate vs. a per-prompt path is never co-located with the scorer or the `run_case` arm description — an implementer cannot determine which case shape triggers each code path.
findings.location: ## What — OUTPUT-SHAPE CONTRACT bullet 2; eval_scorers.py FIRE_EXPECTS line 32

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
